one_line_takeaway: A comprehensive taxonomy-plus-benchmark of 100+ transfer-based adversarial attacks that, under one standardized protocol, demonstrates many newer attacks fail to beat older baselines (e.g., AI-FGTM's 36.3% avg ASR vs VMI-FGSM's 52.7%), exposing widespread unfair comparisons in the field.
key_finding: See structured key_finding above — anchored on Table II average ASR (VMI-FGSM 52.7% vs AI-FGTM 36.3%) and Table VIII targeted averages (L2T 56.6% top-1, SU 10.8%, IDAA 8.7%).
core_contribution: This paper is a survey plus a unified empirical benchmark of transfer-based adversarial attacks on image classification (the associated code is the "TransferAttack" toolbox at github.com/Trustworthy-AI-Group/TransferAttack). Its own contributions are: (1) a taxonomy that organizes 100+ transfer-based attacks (both untargeted and targeted) into six categories — gradient-based, input-transformation-based, advanced objective function, generation-based, model-related, and ensemble-based; (2) a standardized evaluation framework that re-runs these attacks under identical conditions; and (3) an evidence-backed critique that many later attacks fail to outperform earlier baselines, revealing pervasive unfair comparisons in the literature. Note: the record's abstract is truncated and opens with "Ghost Networks [72]…", which is a CATALOGUED/cited method (an ensemble attack), NOT this paper's own contribution; likewise VMI-FGSM [47], MI-FGSM [45], etc. are methods being benchmarked, not proposed here.
threat_model: Black-box transfer attack: the attacker has NO access to the victim/target model (no query, gradients, parameters, or architecture at attack time). The attacker crafts adversarial examples on a locally-owned surrogate model (default RN-50) under an L-inf perturbation budget (epsilon=16/255) and relies on cross-model transferability so those examples also fool the unknown victim model. Both untargeted (any misclassification) and targeted (force a chosen label) objectives are considered; victims include standardly-trained CNNs/ViTs and defended models.
limitations: Scope constraints stated/evident: (1) the benchmark compares attacks only WITHIN each of the six categories — combinations across categories are explicitly out of scope; (2) evaluation is confined to a single 1,000-image ImageNet-compatible dataset at one perturbation budget (epsilon=16/255, L-inf), so budget/dataset sensitivity is not swept; (3) the default surrogate is largely a single model (RN-50), which the paper itself flags as a source of biased assessment; (4) it is a survey/benchmark, not a new attack or defense — it proposes no novel method; (5) some 'best' claims (e.g., MEF as top gradient-based ASR) are asserted in prose without a per-cell figure surfaced in the paper; (6) coverage beyond image classification (object detection, LLMs, cross-task) is described as only a brief overview, not benchmarked.

datasets_used: ImageNet-compatible dataset (1,000 images, 299x299 resized to 224x224, with ground-truth and target labels)
quantitative_results: Gradient-based avg ASR across all victim models (Table II): FGSM 30.3%, I-FGSM 27.7%, MI-FGSM 38.3%, NI-FGSM 40.0%, PI-FGSM 44.7%, VMI-FGSM 52.7%, EMI-FGSM 54.7%, AI-FGTM 36.3%, RAP 53.6%, SMI-FGRM 53.9%

Paper states AI-FGTM and AdaMSI-FGM do NOT outperform the VMI-FGSM baseline (36.3% vs 52.7% avg), and some input-transformation attacks do not beat the DEM baseline — its core unfair-comparison finding
Targeted input-transformation avg ASR (Table VIII): L2T 56.6% (top-1), Everywhere 41.3%, OPS 37.7%, DeCoWA 23.8%, BSR 23.6%, ODI 21.9%, STM 17.6%, SU 10.8%, IDAA 8.7%
Setup: perturbation L-inf epsilon=16/255, step size 1.6/255, T=10 iterations (untargeted) / T=300 (targeted); MI-FGSM default backbone
Taxonomy covers 100+ transfer-based attacks in 6 categories; victim suite = 4 CNNs + 4 ViTs + 5 defenses (AT, HGD, RS, NRP, DiffPure)
